universities.name: Harvard University

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED commitment is extremely valuable—roughly 5.2x RD acceptance rate. ~35% of class fills via ED.

engineering.notes: SEAS accepts ~4% as standalone; strategic recruiting for female engineers

business.notes: HBS is grad-only; undergrad business coursework through Economics concentration

cs.notes: Joint concentrations in CS + another field are competitive; no pure CS major (CS is secondary field)

universities.strategic_insight: ED is the most critical lever here—students not choosing ED sacrifice ~80% of already minuscule chances. Harvard's hidden disadvantage: legacy preference is meaningful but less impactful than peer schools; strong first-gen support exists but admit rate for non-legacy/non-URM is under 2%. Focus RD applications on rare, demonstrable leadership or research impact that's genuinely uncommon.

universities.name: Yale University

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED boost is substantial (~4.5x RD rate). ~25-30% of class from ED.

engineering.notes: Engineering & Applied Science school enrolls ~10% of undergrads; slightly less selective than general admit but still highly competitive

business.notes: No undergrad business school; economics concentration is proxy; SOM is grad-only

cs.notes: Computer Science is increasingly popular but not a separate school; applies to College admissions

universities.strategic_insight: Yale's residential college system is not just housing—it creates subculture identity and affects social experience significantly. Legacy preference is more explicit here than Harvard; approximately 20-25% of admits have family ties. For RD, extreme differentiation is required; 'why Yale' essays must reference specific residential college, specific programs (Yale-NUS, SOAS exchange, drama scene), and genuine engagement. Environmental Studies and Drama are less-reported strengths that may have slightly better odds for non-legacy applicants.

universities.name: Princeton University

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED acceptance is ~9x RD rate—most extreme ED advantage in Ivy League. ~33% of class from ED.

engineering.notes: School of Engineering is more selective than general admission (~2.5% vs 3.1%); strong feeder to tech

business.notes: No undergrad business school; no business concentration. Economics is standard proxy; PWP is grad-only

cs.notes: CS is top-tier selective program (~2.4% acceptance within Engineering discipline)

universities.strategic_insight: ED is mandatory strategy here—RD odds are under 2%, making non-binding ED or RD essentially a lottery. Princeton's engineering school is genuinely more selective; if you're borderline for general admission but stronger in STEM, applying to Engineering is counterintuitive tactical play. The residential college system is real but less pronounced than Yale; physics and math departments are world-class and offer research opportunities for undergrads that rival PhD programs at many universities.

universities.name: Columbia University

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED boost is ~5.2x RD acceptance. ~30% of class from ED.

engineering.notes: Fu School of Engineering is roughly equivalent selectivity to general Columbia; no meaningful separate advantage/disadvantage

business.notes: CBS is grad-only; Economics major is common proxy but no undergrad business school

cs.notes: CS major available through School of Engineering; competitive but slightly less selective than engineering overall

universities.strategic_insight: Columbia's Core Curriculum is non-negotiable and must be genuinely appealing in your application—too many applicants claim to love it without specificity. Location in Manhattan is major draw but also a double-edged sword; housing shortage, expensive, but unparalleled internship access. Engineering applicants should know Fu is smaller and somewhat less prestigious than Ivy peer engineering schools, creating potential opportunity for stronger engineering students. Journalism interest is common but overplayed; SIPA is grad-only despite high visibility.

universities.name: University of Pennsylvania

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED boost is ~7x RD acceptance—among the strongest ED advantages. ~35% of class from ED.

engineering.notes: Penn Engineering accepts ~7.3% vs 3.6% overall—significantly less selective than general admission. Major opportunity for engineering-focused applicants

business.notes: Wharton accepts ~8.3% vs 3.6% overall—even less selective than Engineering. Earliest possible application is ideal; very popular but genuinely more accessible than general admit

cs.notes: CS within Engineering; not separately tracked but ~6% estimated acceptance. Wharton also offers Digital Entrepreneurship track

universities.strategic_insight: Penn's major secret: Wharton and Engineering are measurably less selective than general admission (3.6%), making them genuine strategic advantages if you fit those schools. Wharton ED acceptance is ~16%, enabling much better odds than general admit RD. However, 'Penn for networking' is insufficient for essays—admissions officers see this constantly. If applying to Wharton, demonstrate specific business acumen (founded company, investment portfolio, business plan with data). Engineering students have best tech recruiting pipeline. Cross-school coursework is relatively easy, enabling some optimization for dual-track students.

universities.name: Brown University

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED advantage is ~6.25x RD rate. ~27% of class from ED.

engineering.notes: School of Engineering accepts ~6.2% vs 5.0% general; slightly less selective. Strong co-op program with Brown corporate partners

business.notes: No separate business school; Business concentration available through other schools

cs.notes: CS concentrations within Engineering and other schools; roughly equivalent to general admit rate

universities.strategic_insight: Brown's Open Curriculum is genuinely unique among Ivies—if you thrive with academic autonomy, this is a real differentiator. However, 'I love creative freedom' is generic; successful essays articulate specific intellectual sequences you want to pursue. Engineering is slightly less competitive; pre-med is a strong pipeline. Brown's yield is lowest of Ivies (70%) because of tufts, Cornell competition; less legacy-heavy than other Ivies, making ED more important for admitted students but slightly better odds overall for non-legacy applicants than peer schools.

universities.name: Dartmouth College

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED advantage is ~6.9x RD rate—significant boost. ~25% of class from ED.

engineering.notes: Thayer School of Engineering accepts ~5.8% vs 4.7% overall; slightly less selective but strong reputation

business.notes: No business school; Tuck is grad-only; economics and business analysis concentrations available

cs.notes: CS emphasis within Engineering; strong offerings but not separately tracked

universities.strategic_insight: Dartmouth's 68% Greek life participation means this is a genuine lifestyle choice, not just weekend option—essays should demonstrate comfort with close-knit, outdoorsy community culture if not authentic. Rural location significantly impacts experience; outdoor access is real differentiator but also requires genuine interest. Quarter system is unusual (4 10-week terms + flexibility) and allows off-campus experiences; if highlighting this, explain your specific term-away plan. Legacy rates are highest among Ivies; first-gen and geographic diversity are real advantages. Engineering at Thayer is reputable but less recruited-from than peer schools.

universities.name: Cornell University

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED advantage is ~5.8x RD rate. ~20% of class from ED. Varies significantly by school.

engineering.notes: College of Engineering accepts ~3.2% vs 7% overall—highly selective. Top-tier program with exceptional recruitment.

business.notes: School of Hotel Administration accepts ~12% vs 7% overall—most accessible Cornell school. Non-traditional but strong outcomes.

cs.notes: CS in Engineering is extremely selective (~2.8%). One of top 3 CS programs in US.

universities.strategic_insight: Cornell is the most variable Ivy—Engineering (3.2%) is ~2.2x more selective than overall rate, while Hotel School (12%) is more accessible. Strategic school choice is critical: if you're strong in STEM, apply Engineering; if more liberal arts inclined, College of Arts & Sciences (6.8%). Ithaca location filters applicants significantly; commitment messaging is important. Cornell is less legacy-driven than other Ivies, making it relatively better for non-legacy applicants. Agricultural programs (hidden gem) and Vet School pathway are less-known options. Yield is lowest among Ivies, indicating admitted students choose elsewhere more often.

universities.name: Stanford University

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: No ED; EA acceptance is ~14% vs 2.3% RD—extreme advantage to EA. ~45% of class from EA.

engineering.notes: School of Engineering accepts ~3.5% vs 3.6% overall; essentially same selectivity as general admission

business.notes: GSB is grad-only; undergrads major in Economics or other fields

cs.notes: CS within Engineering is extremely selective (~2.6%); one of most competitive CS programs in world

universities.strategic_insight: Stanford's EA (not ED) is critical: 14% EA vs 2.3% RD makes RD odds effectively lottery (1/43). EA application is almost mandatory. Stanford's location in Silicon Valley drives recruiting—tech companies recruit heavily, and proximity creates internship advantage. CS is exceptionally selective (2.6%), making it only viable for genuine top-tier applicants; other engineering disciplines more accessible. Test-optional is real (though most admits submit); first-gen, geographic diversity (especially non-CA), and URM status are meaningful advantages. Entrepreneurship is normalized here—side projects and execution matter more than other schools.

universities.name: Massachusetts Institute of Technology

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED acceptance is ~4.3x RD rate. ~25% of class from ED.

engineering.notes: Engineering dominates MIT culture; all disciplines (mechanical, electrical, civil, etc.) are world-class

business.notes: Sloan is grad-only; undergrads pursue engineering + management or economics

cs.notes: CS is among most selective MIT programs (~1.8%); exceptional depth of study

universities.strategic_insight: MIT admits almost exclusively based on math/science ability + demonstrated problem-solving; essays and ECs are secondary unless they show genuine technical depth. Legacy is less meaningful here than other elites; gender (female in STEM) is genuine advantage. ED is strategic if committed; RD odds are 2.3%, essentially lottery. Residential system is tight-knit with strong house identity; fraternities/sororities are prominent (33%) but optional. Different from other elites: MIT culture celebrates hard work and nerdy depth; applicants should emphasize genuine curiosity in technical problems, not prestige. First-gen and geographic diversity are appreciated advantages.

universities.name: California Institute of Technology

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED boost is ~4x RD rate. ~15% of class from ED.

engineering.notes: Engineering is equivalent selectivity to overall; all concentrations (mechanical, electrical, civil, chemical) world-class

business.notes: No business school; economics available but not business-focused

cs.notes: CS concentration within Engineering; extremely competitive (~2%)

universities.strategic_insight: Caltech is narrower mission than MIT—heavy physics/astronomy/engineering focus, lighter on humanities/social sciences. Essays must demonstrate genuine passion for fundamental physics or engineering; admissions reject 'smart kids who like science' in favor of problem-solvers obsessed with theory or building. Greek life is ubiquitous (60%); social life revolves around houses; applicants uncomfortable with this should reconsider. Unique advantage: undergraduates conduct research directly with faculty starting freshman year; if you've done physics/engineering research, emphasize that heavily. No legacy preference reported; gender (female in STEM) is advantage. Smallest student body of elite colleges creates unique culture—not for everyone.

universities.name: Duke University

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED boost is ~5.5x RD rate. ~33% of class from ED.

engineering.notes: Pratt School of Engineering accepts ~3.9% vs 4.4% overall; roughly equivalent selectivity

business.notes: Fuqua is grad-only; undergrads pursue business concentration or dual-degree path

cs.notes: CS through Pratt Engineering; competitive but not separately tracked

universities.strategic_insight: Duke's yield is 70%—lower than peer schools—making admitted students more likely to choose elsewhere; this suggests legacy-heavy admits. Non-legacy ED is strategic lever here. Strong pre-med pipeline and medical school acceptance rates; if pre-med, Duke is legitimate advantage. Trinity College (Arts & Sciences) and Pratt (Engineering) have different cultures; Trinity is more traditional liberal arts, Pratt more pre-professional. Athletics culture is significant (45% Greek, sports-heavy); if you're not into Duke basketball or partying, messaging should reflect alternative social channels. Biomedical Engineering is increasingly popular; other engineering disciplines have better accessibility. Ranked highly but attracts fewer RD admits, suggesting ED is more important than at peer institutions.

universities.name: Johns Hopkins University

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED boost is ~3.6x RD rate. ~25% of class from ED.

engineering.notes: Whiting School of Engineering accepts ~6.2% vs 5.8% overall; slightly less selective

business.notes: No undergrad business school; Carey is grad-only

cs.notes: CS within Engineering; roughly equivalent to engineering acceptance rate

universities.strategic_insight: JHU's yield is lowest among peer schools (60%), indicating admitted students frequently choose alternatives; pre-med reputation is accurate but also commodifies application pool. Pre-med and engineering applicants have better outcomes; liberal arts applicants should find other schools. Medical school acceptance rates are strong; if pre-med, JHU is legitimate pipeline. Baltimore location is urban but less attractive than top-tier cities; this filters applicants, making RD odds slightly better than at peer schools (4.2% vs 2.3% at Stanford). Research opportunities are genuine—undergrads can join faculty labs easily. Bloomberg School of Public Health is grad-only but creates health/science ecosystem. Whiting Engineering is accessible for students who don't qualify for MIT/Caltech.

universities.name: Northwestern University

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED boost is ~5.7x RD rate. ~35% of class from ED.

engineering.notes: McCormick School of Engineering accepts ~4.8% vs 6.3% overall; more selective than general admission

business.notes: Kellogg is grad-only; Medill dominates undergrad business/media interests

cs.notes: CS through McCormick; slightly less selective than overall engineering

universities.strategic_insight: Northwestern is less selective than peer Ivies (6.3% vs 3-4%) but highly strategic by school: McCormick Engineering (4.8%) is more selective than overall admit, while Medill (communication/journalism) and SESP (education/social policy) are less selective than general admission. School choice matters significantly. Communication/Medill is major draw; 'I love journalism' is common, so specificity is crucial—what beats? What story will you pursue? ED is valuable (21% ED vs 3.7% RD); non-ED applicants at significant disadvantage. Evanston location is suburban but near Chicago; access to internships is meaningful. Yield is 68%, lower than peer schools, suggesting legacy-heavy admits. Geographic diversity and first-gen status are relative advantages.

universities.name: University of Chicago

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED boost is ~4.3x RD rate. ~30% of class from ED.

engineering.notes: No standalone engineering school; engineering coursework available through College

business.notes: Booth is grad-only; undergrads pursue economics concentration

cs.notes: CS available within College; strong reputation, roughly equivalent selectivity to general admission

universities.strategic_insight: UChicago's culture is intense intellectual rigor—'test optional' here is ironic because success requires proof of quantitative ability. Economics pipeline is exceptional; if interested in econ, this is legitimate advantage. The core curriculum and 'where fun goes to die' reputation are real; essays must demonstrate genuine love of intellectual difficulty, not just prestige-seeking. Uncommon supplement essays require genuine intellectual reflection; generic 'challenge' essays fail. First-gen and international student support is strong, making these groups advantaged. Physics is world-class but less of recruiting pipeline than economics. D3 athletics means no scholarship advantage but also no football culture distortion. Yield is 72%; less legacy-heavy than peer institutions in our sense, making ED slightly more valuable strategically.

universities.name: Georgetown University

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED boost is ~4x RD rate. ~22% of class from ED.

engineering.notes: No engineering school at undergraduate level

business.notes: McDonough School of Business accepts ~9.8% vs 6.2% overall—more accessible than general admission

cs.notes: No standalone CS major; computer science available through College

universities.strategic_insight: Georgetown's acceptance rate varies dramatically by school: McDonough (9.8%) is significantly less selective than College of Arts & Sciences (3.8%), School of Foreign Service (4.1%), or Nursing (14%). If business-inclined, McDonough is strategic advantage. Washington DC location is major draw; internship access is unparalleled for government/policy, making that narrative genuinely valuable. International Relations and Government are dominant; essays claiming IR interest are common and frequently lack depth—specificity is critical. Yield is strong (74%); legacy is meaningful but less extreme than top Ivies. Catholic identity is present but not restrictive. Pre-med pipeline exists but less prominent than Duke/JHU.

universities.name: Vanderbilt University

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED boost is ~4.6x RD rate. ~30% of class from ED.

engineering.notes: School of Engineering accepts ~9.2% vs 7.1% overall—less selective than general admission

business.notes: Owen is grad-only MBA; undergrads pursue finance/business concentrations

cs.notes: CS within Engineering; roughly equivalent to engineering acceptance rate

universities.strategic_insight: Vanderbilt's engineering school (9.2%) is less selective than overall admission (7.1%), making it a strategic advantage for engineering-interested applicants—unusual among peer schools. ED is important (20.5% ED vs 4.5% RD); non-ED applicants face significant disadvantage. Nashville location is distinctive; if you engage with music/culture honestly, that resonates. SEC athletics culture is strong and divisive—must assess fit carefully. Yield is 72%, moderate for this tier. More regionally concentrated (Southeast) than peer institutions; geographic diversity (especially non-South) is relative advantage. Merit aid is available; some applicants qualify for automatic scholarships based on test scores, reducing need-based aid competition. Pre-med outcomes are strong; nursing school feeds into MD pathway.

universities.name: Rice University

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED boost is ~4.6x RD rate. ~25% of class from ED.

engineering.notes: School of Engineering accepts ~6.2% vs 7.3% overall; slightly more selective than general admission

business.notes: Jones is grad-only; undergrads pursue economics or business concentrations

cs.notes: CS within Engineering; competitive but roughly equivalent to engineering rate

universities.strategic_insight: Rice is exceptional value—smallest elite school we cover with 6:1 student-faculty ratio, exceptional outcomes, yet less competitive than peer schools (7.3% vs 3-4% at top Ivies). Engineering is strong but slightly more selective than general; CS is competitive but accessible relative to MIT/Caltech. Houston location is underrated; energy sector recruiting is genuine, and tech companies increasingly recruit here. ED is strategic (20.5% ED vs 4.5% RD); ED students fill 25% of class. Hispanic representation (20%) is highest among elite schools. Residential college system creates strong community. Yield is 71%, solid for tier. Less regionally concentrated than Vanderbilt, more geographically diverse applicant pool. 'Why Rice' essays often lack specificity; residential college, engineering focus, and Houston tie-ins should be detailed.

universities.name: University of Notre Dame

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED boost is ~4.1x RD rate. ~28% of class from ED.

engineering.notes: College of Engineering accepts ~5.4% vs 6.3% overall; slightly more selective

business.notes: Mendoza College of Business accepts ~10.2% vs 6.3% overall—significantly less selective, strategic advantage

cs.notes: CS within Engineering; competitive but available

universities.strategic_insight: Notre Dame's Mendoza School of Business (10.2%) is significantly less selective than general admission (6.3%), making it a clear strategic advantage for business applicants. Catholic identity is core and non-negotiable; applicants uncomfortable with religious affiliation should avoid. No Greek life (0%) means social life built on dorms, clubs, faith community; assessment of fit is critical. Rural location (South Bend is small) is constraint; outdoor activities and tight campus community are reality. Football culture dominates weekends and donor relations; awareness required but doesn't require sports interest. ED is valuable (18.5% ED vs 4.5% RD). Yield is 70%, strong for tier. Legacy is meaningful. Engineering outcomes are strong; pre-med pipeline exists. First-gen support is improving but still less prominent than at peer institutions.

universities.name: Emory University

admissions.ed_advantage_notes: ED boost is ~4.9x RD rate. ~30% of class from ED.

engineering.notes: No engineering school; engineering coursework available through College

business.notes: Goizueta School of Business accepts ~7.7% vs 7.4% overall; roughly equivalent selectivity

cs.notes: CS available through College; competitive but roughly equivalent to general admission

universities.strategic_insight: Emory is least competitive school in our 20-school list (7.4% overall), making it strategic fallback even for very strong applicants. Pre-med and medical school outcomes are strong; medical school acceptance rates exceed peer institutions. ED is highly valuable (24.5% ED vs 5% RD)—non-ED applicants face material disadvantage. Atlanta location offers internship and recruiting access; CDC proximity is unique advantage for public health/medicine students. Yield is lowest in our cohort (62%), indicating admitted students often choose elsewhere; legacy is likely concentrated. Goizueta is roughly equivalent selectivity to general admission (no advantage/disadvantage). Jewish identity is significant part of community culture but not restrictive. D3 athletics means no football distractions. First-gen and racial diversity are relative strengths compared to other elite schools.
